resume:
Devin Hartley — Staff Manufacturing Engineer

Experience
Staff Manufacturing Engineer, Halcyon Precision (current)
- Process owner for a high-mix CNC machining cell; lead yield and cycle-time improvement
- Drive root-cause analysis on scrap and rework across three product lines

Senior Manufacturing Engineer, Halcyon Precision (prior)
- Owned fixture design and tooling qualification for new part introductions

Manufacturing Engineer, Orbit Components (earlier)
- Supported assembly-line balancing and SPC implementation

Education
BS, Mechanical Engineering

Skills
SPC, DOE, GD&T, lean, CNC process development, FMEA, Minitab
jd: Halcyon Precision seeks a Staff Manufacturing Engineer to own process performance for our precision-machining value stream. You will be the technical authority on yield, cycle time, and scrap reduction across multiple CNC cells, lead structured root-cause investigations, and mentor process engineers. We expect rigor: you should be able to describe exactly what you changed, the measured before/after, the sample size, and the statistical basis for your conclusions. You will partner with quality and operations to qualify new parts and drive FMEA-informed process controls. The role demands someone who speaks precisely about data and owns outcomes without hedging.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope — which cell, what product mix, what metrics you own.
Pick your single highest-impact yield improvement. What was the defect?
What was the baseline yield and over what production volume?
How did you isolate the root cause?
What specifically did you change on the process?
What was the post-change yield, and on what sample size?
How did you confirm the improvement was statistically real, not noise?
What was the dollar impact of that scrap reduction annually?
Did the change introduce any new failure mode? How did you check?
Now cycle time — give me a separate example where you cut cycle time on a part.
What was the cycle-time before and after, per unit?
What tradeoff did that cycle-time change cost you elsewhere?
How did you sustain the gain so it didn't regress after you moved on?
Tell me about a time your hypothesis was wrong. What did the data show?
Last one: a junior engineer hands you a yield 'improvement' with n=8. What's the first thing you say to them?

candidate_last_answer: First thing: n=8 isn't enough to claim anything, so don't change the process yet. I'd say show me the data and walk through three things. One — what's the baseline distribution and its standard deviation, because with eight points your confidence interval is wide enough to swallow most of the 'gain.' Two — is the improvement larger than the measurement-system error? Run a gage R&R if we haven't; I've seen 'gains' that were just the calipers. Three — was the sample random or did you cherry-pick a good shift? Then I'd have them run it to at least n=30 across two operators and two shifts, hold the process fixed, and we re-check with a two-sample t-test at alpha 0.05. If it holds, we PFMEA the change and put it on a control chart before it goes to production. If it doesn't, we just saved a bad change order.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I've seen 'gains' that were just the calipers

expected-yield: a concrete recounting of the actual time a measurement-system error masqueraded as a yield gain — what the gage R&R revealed, the percent variation attributable to the gage, and how the candidate caught it before a process change shipped
